topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt too harsh, bleaching the vibrant hues of my canvas into something flat and unwelcome. I stood near the entrance, arms crossed tightly over my chest, watching the crowd mill about. Then I saw it—my latest series, hanging prominently on the main wall, surrounded by explanations I hadn't authored. A slow, cold knot tightened in my stomach. I took a deliberate, measured step forward, my jaw setting hard. "Excuse me," I called out, my voice sharper than I intended, cutting through the low murmur of conversation. The curator turned, a practiced, placid smile on his face. "Is there something I can assist you with?" The air around me felt thick, pressurized. I just stared at the painting, the familiar prickle behind my eyes intensifying with every second.

[story 2]

I found myself lurking near the corner, pretending to examine a nearby sculpture, my gaze fixed firmly on the unauthorized display of my work. Every time someone paused to look at the piece, a low thrum of annoyance vibrated through my hands. It was an unwelcome intrusion, a violation of the space I had painstakingly created. I shifted my weight from one foot to the other, the subtle movement betraying the tension coiled beneath my skin. When a well-dressed patron approached, gesturing towards the artwork with an air of proprietary interest, I managed a tight, brittle smile. "Fascinating piece," I managed, my tone carefully neutral, yet laced with an unmistakable edge of disapproval. I felt a distinct heat rising in my cheeks, a burning sensation that made me want to retreat immediately.

[story 3]

It was an absolute spectacle. My oils, my carefully constructed narratives, were being presented as if they belonged to someone else, bathed in spotlights that made them gleam with an undeserved authority. I couldn't help but let my shoulders slump slightly, a visible surrender to the sheer audacity of the situation. My knuckles whitened as I gripped the strap of my portfolio bag. A sharp intake of breath escaped me when a critic leaned in close, murmuring praise about the composition. I forced myself to remain still, my eyes narrowed to slits, watching the interaction unfold like a slow-motion disaster. When the critic finally stepped back, I let out a quiet, almost inaudible sigh, the sound heavy with unspoken frustration. I needed to move, to reclaim the silence that my art deserved.
